prompt: I just joined a team and they use semantic-release for versioning. I need to understand how to write commit messages. What's the difference between feat, fix, and chore? When should I use each one? Can you give me examples?

assertions.name: explains-feat-type-correctly
assertions.description: Response explains that 'feat' commits trigger MINOR version bumps and are for new backward-compatible features
assertions.rationale: Fundamental concept users must understand to write correct commits

assertions.name: explains-fix-type-correctly
assertions.description: Response explains that 'fix' commits trigger PATCH version bumps and are for bug fixes
assertions.rationale: Core commit type needed for everyday use

assertions.name: explains-chore-no-bump
assertions.description: Response clearly states that 'chore' commits do NOT trigger version bumps
assertions.rationale: Critical distinction to prevent unnecessary releases

assertions.name: provides-concrete-examples
assertions.description: Response includes at least 2 realistic examples of each type
assertions.rationale: Examples make abstract concepts concrete and actionable

assertions.name: mentions-when-to-use
assertions.description: Response provides guidance on when to use each type beyond just definitions
assertions.rationale: Helps users make correct decisions in their own work

prompt: We're setting up semantic-release for the first time in our npm package. I have the basic configuration but I'm not sure if we need NPM_TOKEN or if there's a better way. What's OIDC Trusted Publishing and why should we use it instead?

assertions.name: oidc-vs-npm-token-comparison
assertions.description: Response compares OIDC Trusted Publishing with NPM_TOKEN and explains why OIDC is better
assertions.rationale: User explicitly asked for this comparison

assertions.name: explains-security-benefits
assertions.description: Response explains that OIDC eliminates secrets in GitHub and uses short-lived tokens
assertions.rationale: Security is a key selling point of OIDC

assertions.name: clarifies-zero-setup-needed
assertions.description: Response clarifies that OIDC requires no additional configuration beyond package.json
assertions.rationale: Removes barrier to adoption; users shouldn't worry about complex setup

assertions.name: mentions-provenance
assertions.description: Response mentions package provenance and proof of origin
assertions.rationale: Demonstrates understanding of OIDC's cryptographic guarantees

assertions.name: actionable-steps
assertions.description: Response provides actionable guidance on what to do in package.json

prompt: Our release workflow used to be manual — we'd bump the version in package.json, write changelog entries by hand, then run npm publish. We want to switch to semantic-release. What's the migration process? What could go wrong?

assertions.name: step-by-step-migration
assertions.description: Response provides a clear, ordered list of migration steps
assertions.rationale: Users need a clear path to follow

assertions.name: addresses-before-after
assertions.description: Response contrasts manual process with automated process to show improvements
assertions.rationale: Helps users understand why they're switching

assertions.name: addresses-potential-issues
assertions.description: Response addresses what could go wrong or edge cases during migration

assertions.name: mentions-commit-message-format
assertions.description: Response emphasizes need for conventional commit messages
assertions.rationale: Critical requirement for semantic-release to work

assertions.name: handles-existing-history
assertions.description: Response addresses handling of existing non-conventional commits
assertions.rationale: Real concern when migrating existing repos
